title: Sort array of 0s, 1s, 2s
description: Sort an array containing only 0s, 1s, and 2s using Dutch National Flag algorithm

examples.explanation: Step 1: Input array: [2, 0, 1, 2, 1, 0]. Step 2: Apply quicksort: Choose pivot (e.g., last element 0). Partition: rearrange so elements <0 go left, >0 go right (none since all >=0), resulting in [0, 2, 1, 2, 1, 0] after swaps. Recursively sort subarrays. Step 3: After full sort, array becomes [0, 0, 1, 1, 2, 2]. Step 4: Output sorted array.

Step 1: Input array: [1, 0, 2]. Step 2: Apply mergesort: Split into [1] and [0, 2]. Sort [0, 2] to [0, 2]. Merge [1] and [0, 2]: compare 1 and 0, add 0; compare 1 and 2, add 1; add 2. Step 3: Result: [0, 1, 2]. Step 4: Output sorted array.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Use a standard sorting algorithm like quicksort or mergesort to sort the array, which works for any values but is inefficient for this specific problem. It is straightforward but ignores the constraint of only three distinct elements.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) for in-place sorts like quicksort, or O(n) for mergesort

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sort Array of 0s, 1s, and 2s Using Brute Force O(n log n)
ladders.desc: Complete solution using a standard sorting algorithm to sort an array containing only 0s, 1s, and 2s.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach to sorting an array of 0s, 1s, and 2s involves using a general-purpose sorting algorithm like quicksort or mergesort. While this problem has the constraint of only three distinct values, the brute force method treats it as a standard sorting problem. The intuition is simple: since any sorting algorithm can order elements, we can apply it directly without leveraging the specific values. This works because sorting algorithms compare elements and rearrange them based on their relative order, which applies perfectly to 0s, 1s, and 2s as they have a natural numerical order (0 < 1 < 2). However, this approach is inefficient for this problem because it ignores the constraint, resulting in O(n log n) time complexity where O(n) solutions exist. The step-by-step algorithm involves: 1) Choose a sorting algorithm (e.g., quicksort for in-place sorting). 2) Apply it to the array, comparing elements using standard comparison operators. 3) The array becomes sorted in ascending order. For time analysis, using an efficient algorithm like quicksort or mergesort gives O(n log n) average time, with quicksort having O(n^2) worst-case but O(n log n) average. Space complexity is O(1) for in-place quicksort or O(n) for mergesort due to auxiliary arrays. This connects to the overall problem by providing a baseline solution that works but is suboptimal, highlighting the need for more specialized algorithms like Dutch National Flag. Common mistakes include using inefficient sorts like bubble sort (O(n^2)) or forgetting to handle edge cases like empty arrays. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def sort_brute_force(arr): arr.sort() # Uses built-in sort (typically Timsort, O(n log n)) or implement quicksort: def quicksort(arr, low, high): if low < high: pi = partition(arr, low, high); quicksort(arr, low, pi-1); quicksort(arr, pi+1, high).

examples.trace: Step 1: Input array: [2, 0, 1, 2, 1, 0]. Step 2: Apply quicksort: Choose pivot (e.g., last element 0). Partition: rearrange so elements <0 go left, >0 go right (none since all >=0), resulting in [0, 2, 1, 2, 1, 0] after swaps. Recursively sort subarrays. Step 3: After full sort, array becomes [0, 0, 1, 1, 2, 2]. Step 4: Output sorted array.

Step 1: Input array: [1, 0, 2]. Step 2: Apply mergesort: Split into [1] and [0, 2]. Sort [0, 2] to [0, 2]. Merge [1] and [0, 2]: compare 1 and 0, add 0; compare 1 and 2, add 1; add 2. Step 3: Result: [0, 1, 2]. Step 4: Output sorted array.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Partition Function in Quicksort
ladders.desc: Implement the partition function used in quicksort to rearrange elements around a pivot.
ladders.explanation: The partition function is the core sub-routine in quicksort that rearranges an array so that all elements less than a pivot come before it, and all elements greater come after it, with the pivot placed in its correct sorted position. The intuition is to select a pivot element (commonly the last element), then iterate through the array, swapping elements to maintain two regions: one for elements less than the pivot and one for others. This works because it progressively builds sorted order by positioning the pivot correctly, enabling recursive sorting of subarrays. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Choose a pivot (e.g., last element). 2) Initialize an index i to track the boundary of elements less than pivot. 3) Iterate through the array from low to high-1. 4) If current element is less than pivot, swap it with element at index i and increment i. 5) After loop, swap pivot with element at index i to place pivot in correct position. 6) Return the pivot index. Time complexity is O(n) for partitioning a subarray of size n, and space is O(1) as it operates in-place. This connects to L0 by being the essential helper in quicksort, which is used in the brute force solution. Common mistakes include incorrect pivot selection leading to unbalanced partitions or off-by-one errors in indices. Real code pattern: def partition(arr, low, high): pivot = arr[high]; i = low - 1; for j in range(low, high): if arr[j] < pivot: i += 1; arr[i], arr[j] = arr[j], arr[i]; arr[i+1], arr[high] = arr[high], arr[i+1]; return i+1.

examples.input: Array: [3, 6, 2, 8, 5] with low=0, high=4, pivot=5
examples.trace: Step 1: Input: [3, 6, 2, 8, 5], pivot=5, i=-1. Step 2: j=0, arr[0]=3 <5, i=0, swap arr[0] with itself: [3,6,2,8,5]. Step 3: j=1, arr[1]=6 >=5, no swap. Step 4: j=2, arr[2]=2 <5, i=1, swap arr[1] and arr[2]: [3,2,6,8,5]. Step 5: j=3, arr[3]=8 >=5, no swap. Step 6: Swap pivot arr[4] with arr[i+1]=arr[2]: [3,2,5,8,6]. Step 7: Return pivot index 2.

examples.input: Array: [1, 0, 2] with low=0, high=2, pivot=2
examples.trace: Step 1: Input: [1, 0, 2], pivot=2, i=-1. Step 2: j=0, arr[0]=1 <2, i=0, swap arr[0] with itself: [1,0,2]. Step 3: j=1, arr[1]=0 <2, i=1, swap arr[1] with itself: [1,0,2]. Step 4: Swap pivot arr[2] with arr[i+1]=arr[2]: no change. Step 5: Return pivot index 2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Comparison-Based Sorting for Three Values
ladders.desc: Understand how comparison operators sort 0s, 1s, and 2s by their natural order.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the brute force approach is that standard comparison-based sorting algorithms rely on comparing elements to determine their relative order. For the values 0, 1, and 2, this works because they have a well-defined numerical order: 0 is less than 1, and 1 is less than 2. When a sorting algorithm compares two elements, it uses operators like '<' or '>' to decide which should come first in the sorted array. The intuition is that any sorting algorithm that can handle arbitrary integers will correctly sort these three values by treating them as ordinary numbers. This is effective because the comparisons are consistent and transitive (e.g., if 0<1 and 1<2, then 0<2). The step-by-step insight: 1) Algorithms like quicksort compare elements during partitioning. 2) Mergesort compares during merging. 3) Each comparison checks if one element is less than another, directly applying to 0,1,2. Time and space analysis depend on the specific algorithm but generally involve O(n log n) comparisons. This connects to L1 by being the fundamental principle that allows partition and other sub-routines to work. Common mistakes include assuming special handling is needed for the values or using custom comparators unnecessarily. Real code pattern: In quicksort, during partition: if arr[j] < pivot: swap. For 0,1,2, this compares numerically.

examples.input: Compare elements in [2, 0, 1] using '<' operator
examples.trace: Step 1: Compare 2 and 0: 2 < 0? False, so 0 should come before 2. Step 2: Compare 2 and 1: 2 < 1? False, so 1 before 2. Step 3: Compare 0 and 1: 0 < 1? True, so 0 before 1. Step 4: Overall order: 0, 1, 2.

examples.input: Mergesort merge of [1] and [0, 2]
examples.trace: Step 1: Compare 1 and 0: 1 < 0? False, so take 0 from second array. Step 2: Compare 1 and 2: 1 < 2? True, so take 1 from first array. Step 3: Take remaining 2 from second array. Step 4: Result: [0, 1, 2].

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Sorting with Divide and Conquer
ladders.desc: Learn how recursive algorithms like quicksort or mergesort break the sorting problem into smaller subproblems.
ladders.explanation: Recursive sorting algorithms, such as quicksort and mergesort, use a divide-and-conquer strategy to sort an array. The intuition is to break the problem into smaller, more manageable subproblems, solve them recursively, and then combine the results. For sorting, this involves splitting the array into parts, sorting each part independently, and merging or rearranging them to get the full sorted array. This works because sorting a smaller array is easier, and the combination step ensures overall order. In quicksort, the divide step is partitioning around a pivot, which places the pivot in its correct position and creates two subarrays to sort recursively. In mergesort, the divide step is splitting the array into halves, sorting each recursively, and then merging the sorted halves. The step-by-step process for quicksort: 1) If array has 0 or 1 elements, it's already sorted (base case). 2) Otherwise, partition the array to get a pivot index. 3) Recursively sort the left subarray (elements before pivot). 4) Recursively sort the right subarray (elements after pivot). Time complexity is O(n log n) on average due to balanced recursion, with space O(log n) for recursion stack in quicksort or O(n) for mergesort's auxiliary arrays. This connects to L2 by providing the structural framework that uses comparisons. Common mistakes include missing base cases leading to infinite recursion or poor pivot selection causing inefficiency. Real code pattern: def quicksort(arr, low, high): if low < high: pi = partition(arr, low, high); quicksort(arr, low, pi-1); quicksort(arr, pi+1, high).

examples.input: Quicksort on [3, 6, 2, 8, 5]
examples.trace: Step 1: Call quicksort(0,4). Partition with pivot 5: [3,2,5,8,6], pi=2. Step 2: Recursively quicksort left [3,2]: partition with pivot 2: [2,3], pi=0; quicksort left [] (base); quicksort right [3] (base). Step 3: Recursively quicksort right [8,6]: partition with pivot 6: [6,8], pi=3; quicksort left [] (base); quicksort right [8] (base). Step 4: Combined: [2,3,5,6,8].

examples.input: Mergesort on [1, 0, 2]
examples.trace: Step 1: Split [1,0,2] into [1] and [0,2]. Step 2: Sort [0,2]: split into [0] and [2], merge to [0,2]. Step 3: Merge [1] and [0,2]: compare 1 and 0, take 0; compare 1 and 2, take 1; take 2. Step 4: Result: [0,1,2].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Swapping Array Elements In-Place
ladders.desc: Perform the fundamental operation of swapping two elements in an array without extra space.
ladders.explanation: Swapping two elements in an array is a basic operation used in many sorting algorithms, including the brute force approach. The intuition is to exchange the positions of two elements to rearrange the array, which is essential for achieving sorted order. This works by using a temporary variable to hold one element's value while overwriting it, then assigning the other element's value to the first, and finally placing the temporary value into the second element. This ensures that no data is lost during the exchange. The step-by-step process: 1) Store the value of the first element in a temporary variable. 2) Assign the value of the second element to the first element. 3) Assign the temporary value to the second element. This operation runs in O(1) time and uses O(1) extra space for the temporary variable. It connects to L3 by being a key action in recursive sorting algorithms like quicksort during partitioning or in mergesort during merging (though mergesort typically uses auxiliary arrays). Common mistakes include forgetting the temporary variable, leading to data loss, or swapping indices incorrectly. Real code pattern: temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[j]; arr[j] = temp; or in Python: arr[i], arr[j] = arr[j], arr[i].

examples.input: Swap elements at indices 0 and 2 in array [3, 6, 2]
examples.trace: Step 1: Original array: [3, 6, 2]. Step 2: temp = arr[0] = 3. Step 3: arr[0] = arr[2] = 2. Step 4: arr[2] = temp = 3. Step 5: Result: [2, 6, 3].

examples.input: Swap elements at indices 1 and 1 in array [1, 0, 2] (no change)
examples.trace: Step 1: Original array: [1, 0, 2]. Step 2: temp = arr[1] = 0. Step 3: arr[1] = arr[1] = 0. Step 4: arr[1] = temp = 0. Step 5: Result: [1, 0, 2] (unchanged).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Learn the basics of arrays, how to access elements by index, and iterate through them.
ladders.explanation: Arrays are fundamental data structures that store a collection of elements in contiguous memory locations, each accessible by an index. The intuition is that an array allows efficient access to any element using its position (index), starting from 0 for the first element. This works because the memory address of an element can be calculated directly from the base address and index, enabling O(1) access time. Understanding arrays is crucial for sorting algorithms, as they operate on these structures by reading, comparing, and swapping elements. The step-by-step basics: 1) An array is defined with a fixed size or dynamically allocated. 2) Elements are accessed using square brackets, e.g., arr[i] gets the element at index i. 3) Iteration through an array is done using loops, such as for i in range(len(arr)): process arr[i]. Time complexity for accessing an element is O(1), and iterating through all elements is O(n). Space complexity is O(n) for storing n elements. This connects to L4 by providing the structure on which swapping and other operations are performed. Common mistakes include accessing out-of-bounds indices (causing errors) or confusing zero-based indexing. Real code pattern: arr = [3, 6, 2]; first_element = arr[0]; for i in range(len(arr)): print(arr[i]).

examples.input: Access elements in array [5, 10, 15] at indices 0, 1, 2
examples.trace: Step 1: Array: [5, 10, 15]. Step 2: arr[0] = 5. Step 3: arr[1] = 10. Step 4: arr[2] = 15. Step 5: Output the values.

examples.input: Iterate through array [1, 0, 2] and print each element
examples.trace: Step 1: Array: [1, 0, 2]. Step 2: Loop i from 0 to 2. Step 3: i=0, print arr[0]=1. Step 4: i=1, print arr[1]=0. Step 5: i=2, print arr[2]=2. Step 6: Output sequence.

approaches.name: Counting Sort O(n)
approaches.summary: Count the occurrences of 0s, 1s, and 2s in a single pass, then overwrite the array with the correct number of each element in order. This is efficient and simple but requires extra space for counts.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) for the count array (fixed size of 3), or O(n) if reconstructing a new array

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sort Array of 0s, 1s, and 2s Using Counting Sort
ladders.desc: Complete solution to sort an array containing only 0s, 1s, and 2s using the Counting Sort approach.
ladders.explanation: The Counting Sort approach for this problem leverages the fact that the array contains only three distinct values (0, 1, and 2). The intuition is simple: instead of comparing and swapping elements like in comparison-based sorts, we can count how many times each value appears, then reconstruct the array in sorted order. This works because we know the exact order we want: all 0s first, then all 1s, then all 2s. By counting occurrences, we avoid unnecessary comparisons and achieve linear time complexity. The algorithm proceeds in two phases: first, traverse the array once to count the number of 0s, 1s, and 2s, storing these counts in a fixed-size array of length 3 (index 0 for 0s, index 1 for 1s, index 2 for 2s). This takes O(n) time and O(1) extra space for the counts. Second, overwrite the original array based on the counts: start by filling the array with 0s until we've placed all counted 0s, then 1s, then 2s. This second pass also takes O(n) time, resulting in overall O(n) time complexity. The space complexity is O(1) for the count array (since its size is fixed at 3, independent of input size), but note that if we were to create a new output array, it would be O(n); here, we modify the input array in-place. Connection to L1: This full solution relies on the key subroutine of counting elements, which is detailed in L1. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors when overwriting the array (e.g., not resetting indices correctly) or misunderstanding the space analysis (the count array is constant space). Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def sortColors(nums): count = [0, 0, 0]; for num in nums: count[num] += 1; idx = 0; for i in range(3): for j in range(count[i]): nums[idx] = i; idx += 1.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize count = [0, 0, 0]. Step 2: Traverse array: num=2 -> count[2]=1; num=0 -> count[0]=1; num=2 -> count[2]=2; num=1 -> count[1]=1; num=1 -> count[1]=2; num=0 -> count[0]=2. Final count = [2, 2, 2]. Step 3: Overwrite array: idx=0; for i=0 (value 0): j=0 -> nums[0]=0, idx=1; j=1 -> nums[1]=0, idx=2; for i=1 (value 1): j=0 -> nums[2]=1, idx=3; j=1 -> nums[3]=1, idx=4; for i=2 (value 2): j=0 -> nums[4]=2, idx=5; j=1 -> nums[5]=2, idx=6. Result: [0, 0, 1, 1, 2, 2].

Step 1: Initialize count = [0, 0, 0]. Step 2: Traverse array: num=1 -> count[1]=1; num=2 -> count[2]=1; num=0 -> count[0]=1. Final count = [1, 1, 1]. Step 3: Overwrite array: idx=0; for i=0: j=0 -> nums[0]=0, idx=1; for i=1: j=0 -> nums[1]=1, idx=2; for i=2: j=0 -> nums[2]=2, idx=3. Result: [0, 1, 2].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Count Occurrences of 0s, 1s, and 2s
ladders.desc: Count how many times each value (0, 1, 2) appears in an array.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the Counting Sort approach: it involves traversing the input array once to tally the frequencies of 0s, 1s, and 2s. The intuition is that by knowing the exact counts, we can later reconstruct the array in sorted order without comparing elements. This works because the problem constraints limit values to three known integers, allowing us to use a fixed-size count array. The algorithm is straightforward: initialize an array 'count' of size 3 with all zeros (index 0 for 0s, index 1 for 1s, index 2 for 2s). Then, iterate through each element in the input array; for each element, use its value as an index to increment the corresponding count. For example, if we encounter a 1, we do count[1] += 1. This takes O(n) time, where n is the array length, and O(1) extra space for the count array (since its size is constant at 3). Connection to L0: This counting step feeds directly into the full solution, as the counts are used to overwrite the array in sorted order. Common mistakes include using the wrong indices (e.g., trying to access count[3] for value 2, but indices should be 0-based) or not initializing the count array to zeros. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def countElements(arr): count = [0, 0, 0]; for num in arr: count[num] += 1; return count.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize count = [0, 0, 0]. Step 2: Traverse array: num=0 -> count[0]=1; num=1 -> count[1]=1; num=2 -> count[2]=1; num=0 -> count[0]=2; num=1 -> count[1]=2. Final count = [2, 2, 1].
Step 1: Initialize count = [0, 0, 0]. Step 2: Traverse array: num=2 -> count[2]=1; num=2 -> count[2]=2; num=2 -> count[2]=3. Final count = [0, 0, 3].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Overwrite Array Based on Counts
ladders.desc: Use frequency counts to fill an array with 0s, then 1s, then 2s in order.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves taking the counts of 0s, 1s, and 2s and using them to reconstruct the array in sorted order. The intuition is that since we know the desired order (all 0s first, then 1s, then 2s), we can simply write the correct number of each value sequentially into the array. This avoids any comparison or swapping, making it efficient. The algorithm: start with an index variable set to 0 to track the current position in the array. Then, for each value i from 0 to 2, write that value into the array count[i] times, incrementing the index after each write. For example, if count[0] = 3, we set the first three elements to 0. This step takes O(n) time in total (since we write n elements) and O(1) extra space (just for the index variable). Connection to L1: This logic relies on the counts computed in the previous level. Common mistakes include not resetting the index correctly between loops or writing values in the wrong order (e.g., starting with 2s). Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def overwriteFromCounts(arr, count): idx = 0; for i in range(3): for j in range(count[i]): arr[idx] = i; idx += 1.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize idx = 0. Step 2: For i=0 (value 0), count[0]=2: j=0 -> arr[0]=0, idx=1; j=1 -> arr[1]=0, idx=2. Step 3: For i=1 (value 1), count[1]=3: j=0 -> arr[2]=1, idx=3; j=1 -> arr[3]=1, idx=4; j=2 -> arr[4]=1, idx=5. Step 4: For i=2 (value 2), count[2]=1: j=0 -> arr[5]=2, idx=6. Result: [0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 2].

Step 1: Initialize idx = 0. Step 2: For i=0, count[0]=0: no writes. Step 3: For i=1, count[1]=0: no writes. Step 4: For i=2, count[2]=3: j=0 -> arr[0]=2, idx=1; j=1 -> arr[1]=2, idx=2; j=2 -> arr[2]=2, idx=3. Result: [2, 2, 2].

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterate and Modify Array Elements
ladders.desc: Traverse an array and update its elements based on a pattern or condition.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves the fundamental skill of iterating through an array and modifying its elements in place. The intuition is that arrays are mutable data structures, and we can change their contents by accessing indices directly. This is essential for the Counting Sort approach, where we overwrite the array with sorted values. The algorithm: use a loop to go through each index of the array, and for each index, assign a new value. In the context of our problem, this is done in two phases: first to count elements (where we read values without modifying), and second to overwrite (where we write new values). Time complexity is O(n) for traversing n elements, and space complexity is O(1) if we modify in-place. Connection to L2: This skill is used in the overwriting step, where we iterate and set elements based on counts. Common mistakes include modifying the array while iterating (which can lead to errors if not careful) or using the wrong indices. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def modifyArray(arr): for i in range(len(arr)): arr[i] = new_value_based_on_some_logic.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize i=0: arr[0] = 0, arr becomes [0, 5, 5]. Step 2: i=1: arr[1] = 0, arr becomes [0, 0, 5]. Step 3: i=2: arr[2] = 0, arr becomes [0, 0, 0]. Result: [0, 0, 0].
Step 1: i=0: arr[0] = 1 + 1 = 2, arr becomes [2, 2, 3]. Step 2: i=1: arr[1] = 2 + 1 = 3, arr becomes [2, 3, 3]. Step 3: i=2: arr[2] = 3 + 1 = 4, arr becomes [2, 3, 4]. Result: [2, 3, 4].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Access and Update Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Read from and write to specific positions in an array using indices.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves using indices to interact with array elements. An array is a data structure that stores elements in contiguous memory locations, and each element can be accessed via its index (starting from 0). The intuition is that arrays provide constant-time access to any element if you know its index, making them efficient for tasks like counting or overwriting. To read an element, use array[index]; to update it, assign a new value: array[index] = new_value. In the Counting Sort approach, this is used in both phases: reading values to count them and writing values to sort the array. Time complexity for a single access or update is O(1), and space complexity is O(1) per operation. Connection to L3: This operation is the foundation for iterating and modifying arrays. Common mistakes include using out-of-bounds indices (e.g., index equal to or greater than array length) or confusing 0-based indexing with 1-based. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: element = arr[0]  # read; arr[0] = 5  # write.

examples.trace: Step 1: Read arr[1] = 20. Step 2: Update arr[1] = 25. Result: arr becomes [10, 25, 30].
Step 1: Read arr[0] = 5. Step 2: Update arr[0] = 8. Result: arr becomes [8, 6, 7].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understand Arrays and Indices
ladders.desc: Learn what an array is and how to use indices to refer to elements.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces arrays, which are fundamental data structures in programming. An array is a collection of elements, all of the same type, stored in contiguous memory locations. Each element has a position called an index, which starts at 0 for the first element, 1 for the second, and so on. The intuition is that arrays allow us to group related data together and access it efficiently by position. For example, if we have an array of numbers [10, 20, 30], the element at index 0 is 10, at index 1 is 20, and at index 2 is 30. This concept is crucial for the Counting Sort approach because we use arrays to store the input values and the count frequencies. Time and space analysis here is basic: accessing an element by index is O(1) time, and the array itself occupies O(n) space for n elements. Connection to L4: This understanding enables the basic operations of reading and writing array elements. Common mistakes include thinking indices start at 1 or confusing arrays with other data structures like lists. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: arr = [0, 1, 2]  # define an array; first_element = arr[0]  # access element at index 0.

examples.input: Array: [5, 10, 15], what is element at index 1?
examples.trace: Step 1: Identify the array: [5, 10, 15]. Step 2: Recall indices start at 0: index 0 -> 5, index 1 -> 10, index 2 -> 15. Step 3: Element at index 1 is 10.

examples.input: Array: [2, 4, 6, 8], what are indices for elements 4 and 8?
examples.trace: Step 1: Array: [2, 4, 6, 8]. Step 2: Index 0 -> 2, index 1 -> 4, index 2 -> 6, index 3 -> 8. Step 3: Element 4 is at index 1, element 8 is at index 3.

approaches.name: Dutch National Flag O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a three-pointer (or partition) approach to sort the array in one pass by swapping elements to group 0s, 1s, and 2s. This is the optimal in-place solution designed specifically for this problem.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sort Array of 0s, 1s, and 2s Using Dutch National Flag Algorithm
ladders.desc: Implement the complete Dutch National Flag algorithm to sort an array containing only 0s, 1s, and 2s in a single pass with constant extra space.
ladders.explanation: The Dutch National Flag algorithm, designed by Edsger Dijkstra, is the optimal in-place solution for sorting arrays with three distinct values (0, 1, 2). The intuition is to partition the array into three regions: 0s on the left, 1s in the middle, and 2s on the right, using three pointers: low (tracking the boundary of 0s), mid (current element being processed), and high (tracking the boundary of 2s). The algorithm processes the array from left to right with the mid pointer. If arr[mid] is 0, swap it with arr[low] to move the 0 to the left region, increment both low and mid. If arr[mid] is 1, it's already in the correct region, so just increment mid. If arr[mid] is 2, swap it with arr[high] to move the 2 to the right region, decrement high (but do not increment mid because the swapped element from high needs to be processed). This ensures all elements are placed in their correct partitions in O(n) time with O(1) space. Common mistakes include incorrect pointer updates after swaps, especially when handling 2s, and not considering edge cases like empty arrays or arrays with only one or two types of values. The algorithm terminates when mid exceeds high, indicating all elements have been processed. This approach is efficient because it avoids unnecessary comparisons and moves each element at most once, making it superior to general sorting algorithms for this specific problem.

examples.trace:
Initial: arr = [2, 0, 1, 2, 1, 0], low = 0, mid = 0, high = 5
Step 1: arr[mid] = 2, swap with arr[high] = 0 → arr = [0, 0, 1, 2, 1, 2], high = 4, mid = 0
Step 2: arr[mid] = 0, swap with arr[low] = 0 (same index) → arr unchanged, low = 1, mid = 1
Step 3: arr[mid] = 0, swap with arr[low] = 0 → arr unchanged, low = 2, mid = 2
Step 4: arr[mid] = 1, no swap, mid = 3
Step 5: arr[mid] = 2, swap with arr[high] = 1 → arr = [0, 0, 1, 1, 2, 2], high = 3, mid = 3
Step 6: mid = 3 > high = 3, stop. Sorted array: [0, 0, 1, 1, 2, 2]

Initial: arr = [1, 0, 2, 1, 0], low = 0, mid = 0, high = 4
Step 1: arr[mid] = 1, no swap, mid = 1
Step 2: arr[mid] = 0, swap with arr[low] = 1 → arr = [0, 1, 2, 1, 0], low = 1, mid = 2
Step 3: arr[mid] = 2, swap with arr[high] = 0 → arr = [0, 1, 0, 1, 2], high = 3, mid = 2
Step 4: arr[mid] = 0, swap with arr[low] = 1 → arr = [0, 0, 1, 1, 2], low = 2, mid = 3
Step 5: arr[mid] = 1, no swap, mid = 4
Step 6: mid = 4 > high = 3, stop. Sorted array: [0, 0, 1, 1, 2]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Three-Pointer Partitioning with Swaps
ladders.desc: Implement the core partitioning logic that uses three pointers (low, mid, high) to rearrange elements into three groups based on their values.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the Dutch National Flag algorithm, focusing on the partitioning process that sorts 0s, 1s, and 2s in a single pass. The intuition is to maintain three regions in the array: a low region for 0s (from index 0 to low-1), a middle region for 1s (from low to mid-1), and a high region for 2s (from high+1 to end). The pointers low, mid, and high are initialized to 0, 0, and n-1, respectively. The algorithm iterates while mid <= high. At each step, it examines arr[mid]: if it is 0, swap arr[mid] with arr[low] to move the 0 to the low region, then increment both low and mid; if it is 1, increment mid (as it's already in the correct region); if it is 2, swap arr[mid] with arr[high] to move the 2 to the high region, then decrement high (but do not increment mid because the element swapped from high needs to be re-evaluated). This ensures that all elements are placed in their correct partitions without extra passes. Time complexity is O(n) as each element is processed at most once, and space complexity is O(1) since only a few pointers are used. This sub-routine connects to the full problem by being the main loop that sorts the array; without it, the algorithm would not function. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle the case when arr[mid] is 2 correctly (not incrementing mid) or incorrect boundary conditions. The pseudocode pattern: while mid <= high: if arr[mid] == 0: swap arr[mid], arr[low]; low++; mid++; elif arr[mid] == 1: mid++; else: swap arr[mid], arr[high]; high--.

examples.input: arr = [2, 0, 1], low = 0, mid = 0, high = 2
examples.trace:
Initial: arr = [2, 0, 1], low = 0, mid = 0, high = 2
Step 1: arr[mid] = 2, swap with arr[high] = 1 → arr = [1, 0, 2], high = 1, mid = 0
Step 2: arr[mid] = 1, no swap, mid = 1
Step 3: arr[mid] = 0, swap with arr[low] = 1 → arr = [0, 1, 2], low = 1, mid = 2
Step 4: mid = 2 > high = 1, stop. Sorted arr: [0, 1, 2]

examples.input: arr = [1, 2, 0], low = 0, mid = 0, high = 2
examples.trace:
Initial: arr = [1, 2, 0], low = 0, mid = 0, high = 2
Step 1: arr[mid] = 1, no swap, mid = 1
Step 2: arr[mid] = 2, swap with arr[high] = 0 → arr = [1, 0, 2], high = 1, mid = 1
Step 3: arr[mid] = 0, swap with arr[low] = 1 → arr = [0, 1, 2], low = 1, mid = 2
Step 4: mid = 2 > high = 1, stop. Sorted arr: [0, 1, 2]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Three Regions with Pointers
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the logic of maintaining three separate regions (0s, 1s, 2s) in an array using pointers to track boundaries.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the Dutch National Flag algorithm involves dividing the array into three regions without using extra arrays. The intuition is to use pointers to represent the boundaries between these regions: low points to the first index after the 0s region (i.e., where the next 0 should go), mid points to the current element being examined, and high points to the last index before the 2s region (i.e., where the next 2 should go). Initially, the 0s region is empty (low = 0), the 1s region is empty (mid = 0), and the 2s region is empty (high = n-1). As we process the array with mid, we expand the regions by swapping elements: when we encounter a 0, we swap it to the low region and increment low; when we encounter a 2, we swap it to the high region and decrement high; 1s are left in place, and mid moves forward. This ensures that at any point, elements before low are all 0s, elements between low and mid-1 are all 1s, and elements after high are all 2s, with mid to high being unprocessed. Time complexity is O(n) since each element is looked at once, and space is O(1). This logic feeds into the key sub-routine by providing the framework for the partitioning loop. Common mistakes include misinterpreting pointer roles or not updating pointers correctly after swaps. The pseudocode pattern: initialize low, mid, high; while mid <= high: if arr[mid] is 0, move to low region; if 1, keep in middle; if 2, move to high region.

examples.input: arr = [1, 0, 2], track regions with pointers
examples.trace:
Initial: arr = [1, 0, 2], low = 0, mid = 0, high = 2
Step 1: arr[mid] = 1, region for 1s expands: mid = 1, regions: 0s: [], 1s: [1], 2s: []
Step 2: arr[mid] = 0, swap with arr[low] = 1 → arr = [0, 1, 2], low = 1, mid = 2, regions: 0s: [0], 1s: [1], 2s: []
Step 3: arr[mid] = 2, swap with arr[high] = 2 (same) → arr unchanged, high = 1, mid = 2, regions: 0s: [0], 1s: [1], 2s: [2]
Step 4: mid = 2 > high = 1, stop. Final regions: 0s at index 0, 1s at index 1, 2s at index 2.

examples.input: arr = [2, 0, 1], track regions with pointers
examples.trace:
Initial: arr = [2, 0, 1], low = 0, mid = 0, high = 2
Step 1: arr[mid] = 2, swap with arr[high] = 1 → arr = [1, 0, 2], high = 1, mid = 0, regions: 0s: [], 1s: [], 2s: [2]
Step 2: arr[mid] = 1, mid = 1, regions: 0s: [], 1s: [1], 2s: [2]
Step 3: arr[mid] = 0, swap with arr[low] = 1 → arr = [0, 1, 2], low = 1, mid = 2, regions: 0s: [0], 1s: [1], 2s: [2]
Step 4: mid = 2 > high = 1, stop. Final regions: 0s at index 0, 1s at index 1, 2s at index 2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Swapping Elements to Move Values to Correct Positions
ladders.desc: Practice swapping elements in an array to relocate specific values (0s, 1s, 2s) to designated sections, a fundamental operation in the partitioning process.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the swap operations used in the Dutch National Flag algorithm to move elements to their correct regions. The intuition is that by swapping elements, we can rearrange the array in-place without needing extra storage. Specifically, when we encounter a 0 at position mid, we swap it with the element at position low to move the 0 to the left region (for 0s). Similarly, when we encounter a 2 at position mid, we swap it with the element at position high to move the 2 to the right region (for 2s). Swaps are efficient O(1) operations that maintain the array's integrity. The key is to understand that after swapping, the element that was at low or high moves to mid, and it needs to be processed in the next step (which is why mid is not incremented when swapping with high). This concept is crucial for the core logic of maintaining three regions. Time complexity for swaps is constant per operation, and overall, they contribute to the O(n) time of the full algorithm. Common mistakes include swapping incorrectly (e.g., swapping with the wrong pointer) or not handling the swapped element's re-evaluation. The pseudocode pattern for a swap: temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[j]; arr[j] = temp. This feeds into the core logic by enabling the movement of elements between regions.

examples.input: arr = [2, 0, 1], swap to move 2 to end and 0 to front
examples.trace:
Initial: arr = [2, 0, 1]
Step 1: Swap arr[0] (2) with arr[2] (1) to move 2 to end → arr = [1, 0, 2]
Step 2: Now arr[0] is 1, but we want 0 at front. Swap arr[0] (1) with arr[1] (0) → arr = [0, 1, 2]
Final: arr = [0, 1, 2], with 0 at index 0, 1 at index 1, 2 at index 2.

examples.input: arr = [1, 2, 0], swap to move 0 to front and 2 to end
examples.trace:
Initial: arr = [1, 2, 0]
Step 1: Swap arr[2] (0) with arr[0] (1) to move 0 to front → arr = [0, 2, 1]
Step 2: Now arr[1] is 2, but we want 1 in middle. Swap arr[1] (2) with arr[2] (1) → arr = [0, 1, 2]
Final: arr = [0, 1, 2], with 0 at index 0, 1 at index 1, 2 at index 2.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through an Array with Pointers
ladders.desc: Learn to traverse an array using pointers (indices) to access and compare elements, a fundamental skill for array manipulation.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves using pointers, typically represented as integer indices, to iterate through an array. The intuition is that pointers allow us to track specific positions in the array, such as the current element being processed (mid), the boundary for 0s (low), and the boundary for 2s (high). By incrementing or decrementing these pointers, we can move through the array efficiently in a single pass. For example, starting with mid = 0, we access arr[mid] to examine the current element; based on its value, we update pointers: increment mid to move to the next element, increment low to expand the 0s region, or decrement high to expand the 2s region. This operation is O(1) per step and overall O(n) for traversing the entire array. It connects to the building block of swapping by providing the mechanism to identify which elements to swap. Common mistakes include going out of bounds (e.g., accessing arr[n] when indices are 0 to n-1) or not updating pointers correctly. The pseudocode pattern: initialize pointers; while condition (e.g., mid <= high): access arr[mid]; update pointers based on logic. This is essential for the Dutch National Flag algorithm as it enables the in-place sorting without extra data structures.

examples.input: arr = [1, 0, 2], iterate with mid pointer from 0 to 2
examples.trace:
Initial: mid = 0
Step 1: Access arr[mid] = arr[0] = 1, update mid = 1
Step 2: Access arr[mid] = arr[1] = 0, update mid = 2
Step 3: Access arr[mid] = arr[2] = 2, update mid = 3
Stop as mid = 3 exceeds array length 3. Accessed elements in order: 1, 0, 2.

examples.input: arr = [2, 1, 0], iterate with low=0, mid=0, high=2, updating based on values
examples.output: Pointers after iteration: low=1, mid=3, high=0
examples.trace:
Initial: arr = [2, 1, 0], low = 0, mid = 0, high = 2
Step 1: arr[mid] = 2, update high = 1 (decrement), mid stays 0
Step 2: arr[mid] = 0 (after swap in full algorithm, but here assume no swap for simplicity), update low = 1, mid = 1
Step 3: arr[mid] = 1, update mid = 2
Step 4: arr[mid] = 0, update low = 2, mid = 3
Stop as mid = 3 > high = 1. Final pointers: low = 2, mid = 3, high = 1.

testCases.input: arr = [0, 1, 2], start mid=0, iterate until mid=3
arr = [2, 0, 1], start mid=0, iterate until mid=3
arr = [1, 2, 0], start mid=0, iterate until mid=3
arr = [0, 0, 0], start mid=0, iterate until mid=3
arr = [1, 1, 1], start mid=0, iterate until mid=3
arr = [2, 2, 2], start mid=0, iterate until mid=3
arr = [0, 2, 1], start mid=0, iterate until mid=3
arr = [1, 0, 0], start mid=0, iterate until mid=3
arr = [2, 1, 1], start mid=0, iterate until mid=3
arr = [0, 1, 0], start mid=0, iterate until mid=3

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Element Access
ladders.desc: Learn the basics of arrays, including how to declare, access, and modify elements using indices, which is prerequisite for any array-based algorithm.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers arrays, a fundamental data structure where elements are stored in contiguous memory locations and accessed via indices starting from 0. The intuition is that an array allows O(1) access to any element if you know its index, making it efficient for algorithms like Dutch National Flag. For example, in an array arr of size n, arr[0] is the first element, arr[1] is the second, and arr[n-1] is the last. You can modify elements by assigning new values, e.g., arr[i] = value. This is crucial for the Dutch National Flag algorithm because we need to swap elements by accessing and changing their positions. Time complexity for access and modification is O(1), and space complexity is O(n) for storing the array. This concept feeds into the basic operation of iterating with pointers, as pointers are indices used to traverse the array. Common mistakes include using indices out of bounds (e.g., arr[n] which causes an error) or misunderstanding zero-based indexing. The pseudocode pattern: declare array arr; access arr[i]; modify arr[i] = newValue. For beginners, practice by creating arrays of 0s, 1s, and 2s and accessing elements at different positions.

examples.input: arr = [0, 1, 2], access arr[0], arr[1], arr[2]
examples.trace:
Array arr has 3 elements: index 0 stores 0, index 1 stores 1, index 2 stores 2.
Step 1: Access arr[0] → value is 0.
Step 2: Access arr[1] → value is 1.
Step 3: Access arr[2] → value is 2.
All accesses are valid as indices are within 0 to 2.

examples.input: arr = [2, 0, 1], modify arr[0] to 0, arr[1] to 1, arr[2] to 2
examples.trace:
Initial arr = [2, 0, 1].
Step 1: Modify arr[0] = 0 → arr becomes [0, 0, 1].
Step 2: Modify arr[1] = 1 → arr becomes [0, 1, 1].
Step 3: Modify arr[2] = 2 → arr becomes [0, 1, 2].
Final array: [0, 1, 2], with each element updated to its sorted position.
